images.caption: Figure 1:System overview of NeMo RL with speculative decoding. The vLLM backend produces rollout trajectories, and the policy model (MegatronLM) runs the verifier forward pass for the GRPO policy loss. When online adaptation is enabled, hidden states and log-probabilities from that same pass are cached and reused by a separate speculative-decoding loss for the draft head through a gradient-detached pathway (.detach()). The detached boundary ensures that draft training does not alter the policy gradient signal.

(a)Generation latency per training step. EAGLE-3 achieves mean speedups of1.54×1.54\times(max1.81×1.81\times) on RL-Think and1.79×1.79\times(max2.85×2.85\times) on RL-Zero. The RL-Zero baseline latency rises sharply in the first∼100{\sim}100steps as the policy transitions from short outputs to longer reasoning traces; the EAGLE-3 gap persists throughout.

(a)Generation latency per training step. EAGLE-3 achieves mean speedups of1.54×1.54\times(max1.81×1.81\times) on RL-Think and1.79×1.79\times(max2.85×2.85\times) on RL-Zero. The RL-Zero baseline latency rises sharply in the first∼100{\sim}100steps as the policy transitions from short outputs to longer reasoning traces; the EAGLE-3 gap persists throughout.

(b)Validation accuracy (AIME-2024) versus training step. The EAGLE-3 and autoregressive curves overlap closely throughout: RL-Think rises from∼0.60{\sim}0.60to∼0.70{\sim}0.70and RL-Zero from∼0.03{\sim}0.03to∼0.33{\sim}0.33, confirming that speculative decoding preserves the optimization trajectory.
